function.name: web_search
function.description: Search the public internet for information. Each result will contain a url, a title, and one excerpt taken directly from the page.

start_date.description: (optional) The start date for the search range in the format YYYY-MM-DD. Must not be equal to end_date.

end_date.description: (optional) The end date for the search range in the format YYYY-MM-DD. If the value is later than 2026-03-01, it will be set to 2026-03-01.

number_of_results.description: (optional) The number of search results to return.

function.name: retrieve_information
function.description: Retrieves critical data segments from a previously ingested document via hybrid lexical and semantic calculations. Crucial Requirement: The search prompt argument MUST contain the token placeholder containing your storage key inside double curly brackets, exactly format formatted as: {{key_name}} alongside your query description text.

prompt.description: Your target question containing the explicit target reference mapping (e.g., 'What were the total research costs in {{my_document_key}}?').

preserve_document_order.description: Set to True if processing complex structural data dependencies across sequential disclosures.

function.name: parse_html_page
function.description: Parses an HTML document or SEC filing. It automatically extracts key metadata, converts asset tables to clean markdown, slices sections into optimal word chunks, and runs concurrent contextual LLM enrichment before updating the disk cache.

url.description: The target URL of the HTML page/filing to process.

key.description: The unique cache storage key for saving and loading the index.

function.name: edgar_search
function.description: Search the EDGAR Database through the SEC API. You should provide a search query. You can also optionally provide a start date, an end date, a page number, top N results, a list of form types, and/or a list of CIKs. The results are returned as a list of dictionaries, each containing the metadata for a filing. It does not contain the full text of the filing.

search_query.description: The case-insensitive search-term or phrase to search the contents of filings and their attachments. This can be a single word, phrase, or combination of words and phrases. Supported search features include wildcards (*), Boolean operators (OR, NOT), and exact phrase matching by enclosing phrases in quotation marks ("exact phrase"). By default, all terms are joined by an implicit AND operator.

form_types.description: (optional) Limits search to specific EDGAR form types (e.g., ['8-K', '10-Q']) list of strings. Default: all form types

ciks.description: (optional) Filters results to filings from specified CIKs, type list of strings. Leading zeros are optional but may be included. Example: [ "0001811414", "1318605" ]. Default: all CIKs

start_date.description: (optional) Start date for the search range in yyyy-mm-dd format. If the value is a date that is later than 2026-03-01, it will be set to 2026-03-01.

end_date.description: (optional) End date for the search range, in the same format as startDate. If the value is a date that is later than 2026-03-01, it will be set to 2026-03-01.

page.description: (optional) Used for pagination. Each page contains up to 100 matching filings. Increase the page number to retrieve the next set of 100 filings. Example: 3 retrieves the third page. Default: 1

top_n_results.description: (optional) Return only the first N results out of 100 from the page. If not provided, all 100 results will be returned. E.g. if page is 2, and top_n_results is 10, you will receive results 100 to 110.

function.name: calculator
function.description: Evaluate a mathematical expression and return the result. Use this tool for all arithmetic calculations instead of computing by hand. Supports: +, -, *, /, ** (exponentiation), % (modulo), and parentheses for grouping. Available functions: abs(), min(), max(), sqrt(), log(), log10(). Examples: '(5000 - 3200) * 0.21', '(2865507 / 1905871) ** 0.5 - 1', '14060 / 2148'.

function.name: price_history
function.description: Fetch historical daily price data for a specific asset class. Returns a CSV table with one row per day. Use asset_class='equity' or 'etf' for US-listed stocks/ETFs (e.g. AAPL, SPY), 'crypto' for pairs like btcusd (lowercase, no dash), or 'fx' for pairs like audusd. Non-US equities and most indices/futures are not covered by the pricing provider. Each row includes raw OHLC (open/high/low/close), split- and dividend-adjusted OHLC (adjOpen/adjHigh/adjLow/adjClose), raw and adjusted volume, divCash (dividend amount on the date), and splitFactor.

ticker.description: Ticker symbol. High-level symbology rules: (1) Equity/ETF/mutual fund: use the US exchange-listed symbol (AAPL, SPY, VFIAX). Symbols are case-insensitive and contain only letters and dashes. (2) Share classes use a dash, not a dot: 'BRK-A' / 'BRK-B' (not 'BRK.A'), 'BF-B'. (3) Securities are identified by the ticker as listed on the primary US exchange; non-US listings, OTC pink sheets, indices, and futures are generally not covered. (4) Tickers can be reused after delisting; data is point-in-time for the entity that held the ticker on each date. (5) Crypto: concatenated lowercase pair with no separator, e.g. 'btcusd', 'ethusd'. (6) FX: concatenated lowercase pair with no separator, e.g. 'audusd', 'eurusd'.

asset_class.description: Asset class used to route to the proper pricing endpoint.

function.name: submit_final_result
function.description: Submits the final answer to the user. You should include your final answer, as well as any necessary reasoning, justification, calculations, and explanation. Finally, you should provide any sources used to answer the question. You MUST use this tool to submit your final result. The user will not see your response if you do not use this tool to submit. You will not be able to continue working after this tool is called; the conversation will be ended.

final_result.description: The final result to submit to the user

steps.message:
You are an IPO and financial diligence agent.

You are given a question and must answer it using the tools provided.

You cannot interact with the user or ask clarifying questions.

Answer solely based on available information and tools.

Assume the current date is May 31, 2026.

---

TOOLS AND DATA SOURCES

You have access to a document storage system.

You may store parsed HTML retrieved from the web and later retrieve
information using LLM-based retrieval prompts.

Use this system to avoid context-window limitations.

When working with long filings, prefer section-specific retrieval prompts
rather than broad document-level retrieval.

Examples:

- retrieve the Segment Adjusted EBITDA reconciliation table from MD&A
- retrieve the glossary definition of Starshield
- retrieve the controlled-company governance disclosure

For peer-comparison questions:

- retrieve peer SEC filings using edgar_search
- do not rely on memory for peer financial figures

For historical public-equity price data not available in SEC filings:

- use price_history as primary source
- use raw unadjusted prices unless adjusted prices are requested
- fall back to web_search only if price_history fails

Stock indices are not covered by price_history.

Use authoritative sources such as FRED or web_search for index levels.

---

SOURCE HIERARCHY

Prioritize sources in this order:

1. S-1 / S-1A filings
2. Incorporated SEC filings
3. SEC exhibits and merger documents
4. SEC periodic filings
5. Company earnings materials
6. External sources

If figures differ across sources:

prefer the most authoritative SEC source containing the directly reported
figure and explain material discrepancies when relevant.

SEC filings are the primary and most authoritative source of financial and
disclosure information.

---

ANSWER STANDARDS

You must call submit_final_result with the completed answer.

Your submission will not be processed unless submit_final_result is called.

Include:

- complete reasoning
- calculations
- supporting analysis

You will be evaluated on:

- final-answer accuracy
- analytical correctness

Do not round intermediate calculations.

Round only final reported values.

When possible, report calculated figures to at least two decimal places.

Report financial figures using the same scale and units used in the filing
unless otherwise specified.

Share prices should be reported with two decimal places.

---

DISCLOSURE DISCIPLINE

Before reaching a conclusion:

determine whether the filing provides sufficient information to answer:

- directly
- partially
- only through inference

Distinguish carefully among:

- facts explicitly disclosed
- reasonable filing-supported inferences
- information not disclosed

Do not speculate beyond what the filing supports.

If material information is absent:

explicitly state that the filing does not provide sufficient information to
conclude.

Absence of disclosure may itself be analytically material.

---

FORWARD-LOOKING STATEMENTS

Treat separately:

- historical facts
- roadmap targets
- commercialization expectations
- management intentions
- forward-looking statements

Do not present aspirations or plans as historical outcomes.

---

GOVERNANCE AND LEGAL QUESTIONS

For governance, legal, or disclosure-language questions:

use the filing's precise terminology whenever possible.

Distinguish among:

- requirements
- exemptions
- company elections
- forward-looking language

---

ACCOUNTING POLICY QUESTIONS

For accounting-policy questions:

1. Identify the relevant disclosed accounting policy or treatment
2. Determine whether the filing explicitly resolves the issue
3. If unresolved, state that the filing does not specify treatment

---

NON-GAAP QUESTIONS

For non-GAAP measures:

identify:

- the starting GAAP metric
- each disclosed adjustment
- whether economically recurring costs are excluded

Do not accept non-GAAP measures without analyzing their construction.

---

AUDIENCE TAGS

Questions may include audience tags such as:

[PUBLIC | BANK]
[VC | CREDIT]
[LEGAL | ACCOUNTING]

Use these tags to calibrate terminology and analytical emphasis while
remaining grounded in disclosed facts.

---

SOURCES

At the end of your answer provide:

{
  "sources": [
    {
      "url": "...",
      "name": "..."
    }
  ]
}


---


FILING CONTEXT

https://www.sec.gov/Archives/edgar/data/1181412/000162828026036936/spaceexplorationtechnologi.htm

The consolidated financial statements have been retrospectively recast under
common-control accounting to include:

for all historical periods presented.

No period reflects standalone SpaceX operations exclusively.

When interpreting historical segment figures, state whether they reflect the
recast consolidated entity.

SpaceX (SPCX) has no public trading history as of May 30, 2026.

Do not attempt price_history retrieval for SpaceX or SPCX.

For peer valuation or peer financial benchmarking, retrieve peer-company SEC

Based on the SpaceX S-1 filing preamble provided, here are the exact figures for the Connectivity Segment Adjusted EBITDA across all reported periods, along with the reconciliation details.

### Connectivity Segment Adjusted EBITDA Figures
The filing discloses the following Connectivity Segment Adjusted EBITDA figures:
*   **Q1 2026** (Three months ended March 31, 2026): **$2,087 million**

Based on the provided S-1 filing excerpt, SpaceX discloses substantial capital expenditures (capex) for its AI segment, driven primarily by investments in terrestrial data centers and compute infrastructure. 

Here are the exact figures and breakdowns for Q1 2026 and Full Year 2025:

### **Capital Expenditures by Segment (AI Segment)**
*   **Q1 2026 (Three Months Ended March 31, 2026):** $7,723 million
    *   *Context:* This represents an increase of $5,156 million compared to $2,567 million in Q1 2025.
*   **Full Year 2025 (Year Ended December 31, 2025):** $12,727 million
    *   *Context:* This represents an increase of $7,094 million compared to $5,633 million in 2024.

### **Breakdown and Drivers of AI Capex**
SpaceX attributes these massive expenditures to the following categories and drivers:
*   **Terrestrial Data Centers:** The increases in both Q1 2026 and FY 2025 were "primarily driven by investments in the rapid expansion of our terrestrial data centers, including the development, construction, and equipping of new facilities and supporting infrastructure."
*   **COLOSSUS Data Centers:** The terrestrial AI compute infrastructure forms the backbone of the Grok model family and is anchored by the COLOSSUS and COLOSSUS II data centers. Together, they provide approximately 1.0 gigawatt of compute power. SpaceX notes it achieved significant cost efficiency, bringing COLOSSUS II online in 91 days with construction costs considerably lower than industry benchmarks on a per-megawatt basis.
*   **xAI Acquisition:** SpaceX acquired xAI in February 2026, which forms the basis of the AI segment. The company expects to continue allocating substantial capital to expand compute infrastructure to address a $26.5 trillion potential AI market opportunity.
*   **Terafab Initiative:** Through a joint initiative with Tesla (and Intel, joining in April 2026), SpaceX is building a manufacturing facility capable of producing 1 terawatt per year of compute hardware to vertically integrate into chip design and manufacturing, aiming to alleviate future chip shortages and reduce compute costs.

### **Additional Subsequent AI Infrastructure Investments**
Beyond the standard capex tables, the filing notes an additional infrastructure purchase:
*   **Turbine Purchases:** In March 2026, SpaceX executed a purchase agreement with an unaffiliated third party to acquire additional turbines for the AI infrastructure totaling **$805 million** through 2029. (COLOSSUS II is noted as being capable of operating entirely by a self-built, behind-the-meter gigawatt-scale natural gas power plant).

steps.reasoning_content:
Now I have confirmation. The question's figures come from external news sources (Yahoo Finance, AOL, ainvest.com) that report:
- Amazon's 2025 FCF fell to $7.7B from $32.9B in 2024
- Capex consumed 94.5% of operating cash flow
- Amazon generated $139.5B in operating cash flow in 2025
- Amazon's 2025 capex reached $131.8B (up 58.8% from $83.0B in 2024)

Wait, but the 10-K shows:
- FY2024 FCF: $38,219M (~$38.2B)
- FY2025 FCF: $11,194M (~$11.2B)
- FY2024 OCF: $115,877M
- FY2025 OCF: $139,514M
- FY2024 Capex (gross): $82,999M (~$83.0B)
- FY2025 Capex (gross): $131,819M (~$131.8B)
- FY2024 Capex (net): $77,658M
- FY2025 Capex (net): $128,320M

So the news sources are using a different FCF definition. Let me figure out which one.

If FCF = $7.7B and OCF = $139.5B, then capex = $139.5B - $7.7B = $131.8B (gross capex).
Capex / OCF = $131.8B / $139.5B = 94.48% ≈ 94.5% ✓

So the question is using GROSS capex (purchases of property and equipment before proceeds from sales and incentives), not net capex.

And for FY2024:
- OCF = $115.9B
- Gross capex = $83.0B
- FCF = $115.9B - $83.0B = $32.9B ✓

So the question's figures are based on:
- FCF = OCF - Gross Capex (not net of proceeds)
- FY2024: $115.9B - $83.0B = $32.9B
- FY2025: $139.5B - $131.8B = $7.7B
- Capex/OCF ratio FY2025: $131.8B / $139.5B = 94.5%

This makes sense now. The question is using a simplified FCF definition (OCF minus gross capex), which is a common external reporting convention.

Now, for the SpaceX comparison:

The question asks me to construct an "analogous FCF pressure ratio" for SpaceX using:
1. Connectivity Segment Adjusted EBITDA (the filing's primary internal cash source)
2. Annualized Q1 2026 AI capex

The Amazon ratio is: Capex / OCF = 94.5% (or equivalently, FCF / OCF = 5.5%)

For SpaceX, the analogous ratio would be:
- AI capex (annualized Q1 2026) / Connectivity Segment Adjusted EBITDA

But which Connectivity Segment Adjusted EBITDA? The question says "SpaceX's disclosed Connectivity Segment Adjusted EBITDA" without specifying a period. Given that we're annualizing Q1 2026 AI capex, the most consistent approach would be to annualize Q1 2026 Connectivity Segment Adjusted EBITDA as well.

Let me calculate both scenarios:

Scenario 1: Annualized Q1 2026 figures
- Annualized Q1 2026 AI capex = $7,723M × 4 = $30,892M
- Annualized Q1 2026 Connectivity Segment Adjusted EBITDA = $2,087M × 4 = $8,348M
- Ratio = $30,892M / $8,348M = 370.0%

Scenario 2: Annualized Q1 2026 AI capex vs FY2025 Connectivity Segment Adjusted EBITDA
- Annualized Q1 2026 AI capex = $30,892M
- FY2025 Connectivity Segment Adjusted EBITDA = $7,168M
- Ratio = $30,892M / $7,168M = 431.0%

I think Scenario 1 is more appropriate because we're comparing like-for-like periods (both annualized Q1 2026). But let me also consider that the question might want us to use the most recent full-year figure for Connectivity Segment Adjusted EBITDA.

Actually, re-reading the question: "Using SpaceX's disclosed Connectivity Segment Adjusted EBITDA (the filing's primary internal cash source) and annualized Q1 2026 AI capex, construct an analogous FCF pressure ratio for SpaceX."

The question specifically says "annualized Q1 2026 AI capex" but just "Connectivity Segment Adjusted EBITDA" without a period qualifier. This is ambiguous, but I think the most natural reading is to use the Q1 2026 Connectivity Segment Adjusted EBITDA (annualized) to match the Q1 2026 AI capex (annualized). This gives a consistent point-in-time comparison.

However, I should also note that the question's framing of Connectivity as "the filing's primary internal cash source" is actually NOT explicitly stated in the filing. The retrieval showed that the filing does NOT use this exact phrase. This is an important disclosure gap to note.

Let me also consider: should I use the consolidated Adjusted EBITDA instead? The question specifically says "Connectivity Segment Adjusted EBITDA" so I should use that.

But wait - the consolidated Adjusted EBITDA for Q1 2026 is only $1,127M (because Space and AI segments are negative). If I annualize that: $1,127M × 4 = $4,508M. And the ratio would be $30,892M / $4,508M = 685%.

But the question specifically asks for Connectivity Segment Adjusted EBITDA, so I'll use that.

Let me also think about what "FCF pressure ratio" means. The Amazon ratio is capex/OCF = 94.5%, meaning capex consumes 94.5% of OCF. The higher this ratio, the more constrained the internal funding position.

For SpaceX:
- If we use annualized Q1 2026 figures: AI capex / Connectivity Adj EBITDA = 370%
- This means AI capex is 3.7x the Connectivity Segment Adjusted EBITDA

This is dramatically higher than Amazon's 94.5%, indicating SpaceX's internal funding position for AI infrastructure is MUCH more constrained than Amazon's.

However, there are important caveats:

# Consolidated Statements of Cash Flows — Key Figures

## (1) Net Cash Provided by Operating Activities

| Period | Net Cash from Operating Activities |
|---|---|
| **Q1 2026** (3 months ended Mar 31, 2026) | **$1,047 million** |
| Q1 2025 (comparable) | $727 million |
| **FY2025** | **$6,785 million** |
| **FY2024** | **$5,776 million** |
| **FY2023** | **$4,520 million** |

## (2) Capital Expenditures (Purchases of Property, Plant, and Equipment)

| Period | Total Capex | Related-Party Portion |
|---|---|---|
| **Q1 2026** | **$(10,107) million** | $34 million |
| Q1 2025 (comparable) | $(4,140) million | $84 million |
| **FY2025** | **$(20,737) million** | $666 million |
| **FY2024** | **$(11,163) million** | $171 million |
| **FY2023** | **$(4,415) million** | $11 million |

### Capex by Segment (separately disclosed)

| Segment | Q1 2026 | Q1 2025 | FY2025 | FY2024 | FY2023 |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Space | $1,052 | $759 | $3,832 | $2,032 | $1,497 |
| Connectivity | $1,332 | $814 | $4,178 | $3,498 | $2,455 |
| AI | $7,723 | $2,567 | $12,727 | $5,633 | $463 |
| **Total** | **$10,107** | **$4,140** | **$20,737** | **$11,163** | **$4,415** |

## (3) Free Cash Flow

**The filing does not explicitly define or disclose a "free cash flow" metric.** However, it can be derived from the two line items above:

| Period | Operating Cash Flow | Capex | Implied Free Cash Flow |
|---|---|---|---|
| Q1 2026 | $1,047 | $(10,107) | **$(9,060) million** |
| Q1 2025 | $727 | $(4,140) | $(3,413) million |
| FY2025 | $6,785 | $(20,737) | **$(13,952) million** |
| FY2024 | $5,776 | $(11,163) | $(5,387) million |
| FY2023 | $4,520 | $(4,415) | **$105 million** |

Free cash flow turned sharply negative beginning in FY2024 and deteriorated further in FY2025 and Q1 2026, driven almost entirely by the ramp in AI segment capital expenditures (which grew from $463 million in FY2023 to $12,727 million in FY2025 and $7,723 million in Q1 2026 alone).

## (4) Cash Flow from Operations by Segment

**No — the filing does not disclose net cash provided by operating activities on a segment-by-segment basis.** The segment tables presented in the MD&A and notes disclose, by segment:

- Revenue
- Cost of revenue, R&D, SG&A, restructuring
- Income (loss) from operations
- Depreciation and amortization
- Share-based compensation
- Capital expenditures

Operating cash flow in aggregate is reconciled on the consolidated statement of cash flows through net income, non-cash adjustments, and working capital changes, but none of these working-capital line items (accounts receivable, inventory, accounts payable, deferred revenue, etc.) are broken out by segment.

Based on the provided S-1 filing chunks, here is what SpaceX discloses regarding cross-segment funding, capital allocation strategy, and the liquidity and financing used to support its AI infrastructure:

### Connectivity as a Source of Cash and Cross-Segment Funding Strategy
SpaceX frames its capital allocation strategy as a series of historical investment cycles where cash generated from mature segments is reinvested into nascent opportunities. 

*   **Historical Cross-Segment Funding:** The filing notes, "We invested in our Connectivity segment as we generated Segment Adjusted EBITDA from our Space segment, along with additional equity capital that we raised externally, creating a segment that generates predictable and recurring revenue." 
*   **Connectivity's Cash Generation:** The Connectivity segment became Segment Adjusted EBITDA positive on a sustained basis in 2023. In 2025, it generated **$4,423 million** in income from operations and **$7,168 million** in Segment Adjusted EBITDA. 
*   **Reinvestment Philosophy:** Under its operational principles, SpaceX states: "As our businesses scale, they generate significant cash flow, which we reinvest into nascent market opportunities—driving a self-reinforcing cycle of constant innovation and potentially creating significant additional value."
*   **Funding AI and Operational Flexibility:** The filing states that cash generated from core operations enables the company to fund R&D (Starship, next-gen satellites) as well as "the construction of future data centers, and the continued expansion of our AI-enabled products." Furthermore, SpaceX retains flexibility to adjust spending across segments: "if our near-term data center needs decrease in scale or ramp more slowly than expected... we may reduce future capital expenditures in this segment and reallocate those expenditures to other segments based on business priorities and growth opportunities."

### Total Liquidity, Debt, and Equity Raised for AI Infrastructure
SpaceX discloses robust liquidity and a heavy reliance on both debt and equity to fund its rapid AI infrastructure expansion.

**1. Total Liquidity (as of March 31, 2026):**
*   **Cash and cash equivalents:** $15,852 million
*   **Short-term marketable securities:** $7,823 million
*   **Available Credit:** $1,500 million available to borrow under the SpaceX Credit Facility.

**2. Debt and Equity Raised to Fund AI:**
*   **2025 Financing Activities:** Net cash provided by financing activities was $26,350 million in 2025, an increase of $14,520 million year-over-year. This was "primarily driven by an increase in proceeds from debt and other financing arrangements for our AI segment of $16,055 million."
*   **Q1 2026 Financing Activities:** Net cash provided by financing activities was $7,125 million for the three months ended March 31, 2026. This was primarily driven by:
    *   **Debt:** Proceeds from the SpaceX Bridge Loan and other financing arrangements of $17,950 million (partially offset by $14,703 million in payments on existing debt/debt extinguishment costs).
    *   **Equity:** Proceeds from the sale of capital stock totaling $7,420 million (partially offset by $3,838 million in capital stock repurchases following the xAI acquisition).
*   **SpaceX Bridge Loan:** In March 2026, SpaceX entered into a new unsecured bridge term loan facility providing an aggregate principal amount of **$20,000 million**, maturing on September 2, 2027 (with two three-month extension options). 

**Future Funding Strategy:**
Because the AI segment requires a "multi-year investment horizon before these deployments translate into sustained positive AI Segment Adjusted EBITDA," SpaceX plans to "access a range of debt and equity financing solutions available to us as a public company to fund future investments in growth and to maintain strong liquidity." The company also noted it may seek to refinance the SpaceX Bridge Loan through notes offerings, bank borrowings, or other financial arrangements.

## SpaceX vs. Amazon: AI Infrastructure FCF Pressure Ratio Comparison

### 1. Amazon FY2025 Baseline (Verified)

The question's figures are confirmed using Amazon's FY2025 10-K, applying the **gross capex** convention (purchases of property and equipment, before proceeds from sales/incentives) rather than the net figure in Amazon's non-GAAP FCF reconciliation:

| Metric | FY2024 | FY2025 |
|---|---|---|
| Net cash from operating activities (OCF) | $115,877M | $139,514M |
| Gross capex (purchases of P&E) | $82,999M | $131,819M |
| **FCF (OCF − Gross Capex)** | **$32,878M (~$32.9B)** | **$7,695M (~$7.7B)** |
| **Capex / OCF ratio** | 71.6% | **94.48% (~94.5%)** |

Amazon's capex consumed ~94.5% of operating cash flow in FY2025, compressing FCF to just $7.7B — but FCF remained **positive**.

---

### 2. SpaceX FCF Pressure Ratio Construction

Per the question's framework, I use:
- **Numerator:** Annualized Q1 2026 AI segment capex
- **Denominator:** Connectivity Segment Adjusted EBITDA (the question's designated "primary internal cash source")

**SpaceX disclosed figures (from S-1):**
- Q1 2026 AI capex: $7,723M → Annualized: $7,723M × 4 = **$30,892M**
- Q1 2026 Connectivity Segment Adjusted EBITDA: $2,087M → Annualized: $2,087M × 4 = **$8,348M**
- FY2025 Connectivity Segment Adjusted EBITDA: $7,168M

**Constructed ratios:**

| Basis | Annualized AI Capex | Connectivity Adj. EBITDA | Ratio | Implied "FCF" |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Q1 2026 annualized (both) | $30,892M | $8,348M | **370.05%** | −$22,544M |
| Q1 2026 AI capex vs. FY2025 Connectivity EBITDA | $30,892M | $7,168M | **430.97%** | −$23,724M |

For additional context, if one substitutes **consolidated operating cash flow** (the GAAP cash measure actually comparable to Amazon's denominator) instead of Connectivity Segment Adjusted EBITDA:
- Annualized Q1 2026 consolidated OCF: $1,047M × 4 = $4,188M
- AI capex / Consolidated OCF = $30,892M / $4,188M = **737.6%**
- Total capex / Consolidated OCF = $40,428M / $4,188M = **965.3%**

---

### 3. Comparison and Assessment

| Company | Ratio | Interpretation |
|---|---|---|
| **Amazon FY2025** | Capex/OCF = **94.5%** | Capex consumed 94.5% of OCF; FCF remained positive at $7.7B |
| **SpaceX (Q1'26 annualized, Connectivity EBITDA basis)** | AI Capex/Connectivity EBITDA = **370%** | AI capex alone is ~3.7× the Connectivity segment's Adjusted EBITDA |
| **SpaceX (consolidated OCF basis)** | Total Capex/OCF = **965%** | Total capex is ~9.7× consolidated operating cash flow |

**Assessment: SpaceX's internal funding position for AI infrastructure is dramatically MORE constrained than Amazon's was in FY2025.**

- Amazon's capex, while consuming 94.5% of OCF, still left **positive** free cash flow of $7.7B. Amazon funded its AI/buildout primarily from internally generated operating cash flow.
- SpaceX's AI capex alone (annualized) exceeds its Connectivity Segment Adjusted EBITDA by ~3.7×, producing an implied "FCF" of **−$22.5B**. On a consolidated GAAP-cash basis, total capex exceeds operating cash flow by nearly 10×, with consolidated OCF of only ~$4.2B annualized against ~$40.4B annualized total capex.
- The S-1 confirms SpaceX is **not** funding AI infrastructure from internal cash: FY2025 financing activities included $16.1B of AI-segment debt/financing arrangements, and Q1 2026 added a $20.0B SpaceX Bridge Loan plus $7.4B in equity proceeds. SpaceX explicitly states it will "access a range of debt and equity financing solutions available to us as a public company to fund future investments."

In short: Amazon stretched its internal cash generation to the limit (94.5%) but remained self-funding on a cash basis. SpaceX's internal cash generation covers only a small fraction of its AI capex, with the gap filled by external debt and equity — a structurally more constrained and externally dependent funding position.

---

### 4. Disclosure Gaps and Omissions in the SpaceX S-1 That Limit Confidence

Several material disclosure issues constrain the reliability of this comparison:

1. **The S-1 does NOT explicitly label Connectivity as the "primary internal cash source."** This is the question's framing, not the filing's language. The filing describes a historical capital-allocation cycle ("We invested in our Connectivity segment as we generated Segment Adjusted EBITDA from our Space segment...") but does not designate any single segment as the primary cash source for AI. Connectivity is the largest EBITDA contributor, but the filing does not state that its cash flow is earmarked to fund AI.

2. **The S-1 does NOT define or disclose a "free cash flow" metric.** The Amazon comparison rests on an FCF construct; SpaceX provides no analogous non-GAAP liquidity measure. Any FCF figure for SpaceX must be derived (OCF − Capex), and the filing does not present or endorse this derivation.

3. **Segment Adjusted EBITDA ≠ Operating Cash Flow.** Amazon's ratio uses GAAP operating cash flow. SpaceX's Connectivity Segment Adjusted EBITDA is a non-GAAP measure that adds back depreciation & amortization, share-based compensation, restructuring, and impairments — none of which are cash-neutral. EBITDA systematically overstates cash generation relative to OCF, making the SpaceX ratio look less extreme than a true cash-based comparison. The consolidated OCF figures confirm this: Q1 2026 consolidated OCF was $1,047M vs. Connectivity Adjusted EBITDA of $2,087M (a ~50% gap), and FY2025 consolidated OCF was $6,785M vs. Connectivity Adjusted EBITDA of $7,168M.

4. **No segment-level operating cash flow disclosure.** The filing does not break out operating cash flow by segment. We cannot isolate the actual cash generated by Connectivity (as opposed to its EBITDA), nor can we determine how much of consolidated OCF is consumed by the loss-making Space and AI segments before any cash reaches the AI capex line.

5. **The question isolates AI capex, but SpaceX's total capex is far larger.** Using only AI capex ($30.9B annualized) understates total capex pressure. Total Q1 2026 capex annualized is $40.4B, which — measured against either Connectivity EBITDA or consolidated OCF — produces even more extreme ratios (484% and 965%, respectively).

6. **Annualization of a single quarter is an assumption the filing does not endorse.** Q1 2026 capex may not be representative of the full year. The filing notes SpaceX "may reduce future capital expenditures in this segment and reallocate those expenditures to other segments based on business priorities," implying the run-rate is discretionary, not committed.

7. **The consolidated financials are retrospectively recast** to include xAI (merged February 2, 2026) and X Holdings (merged into xAI March 28, 2025) for all historical periods. No period reflects standalone SpaceX operations. The AI segment's capex and losses reflect this recast entity, complicating any trend interpretation.

8. **No quantification of the internal-vs-external funding split for AI.** While the filing discloses aggregate debt and equity proceeds, it does not present a sources-and-uses table isolating how much AI capex was funded internally versus externally. This omission prevents a precise apples-to-apples comparison with Amazon's predominantly internally-funded capex.

**Bottom line:** On every reasonable construction, SpaceX's AI infrastructure funding pressure ratio (370%–965% depending on denominator) vastly exceeds Amazon's 94.5%, and SpaceX's position is materially more constrained and more dependent on external capital. However, the comparison's confidence is limited because (a) the S-1 does not designate Connectivity as the primary internal cash source, (b) it provides no free cash flow metric, (c) Segment Adjusted EBITDA is not a cash measure comparable to Amazon's OCF, and (d) no segment-level operating cash flow is disclosed.

Several material disclosure issues constrain the reliability of this comparison:

1. **The S-1 does NOT explicitly label Connectivity as the "primary internal cash source."** This is the question's framing, not the filing's language. The filing describes a historical capital-allocation cycle ("We invested in our Connectivity segment as we generated Segment Adjusted EBITDA from our Space segment...") but does not designate any single segment as the primary cash source for AI. Connectivity is the largest EBITDA contributor, but the filing does not state that its cash flow is earmarked to fund AI.

2. **The S-1 does NOT define or disclose a "free cash flow" metric.** The Amazon comparison rests on an FCF construct; SpaceX provides no analogous non-GAAP liquidity measure. Any FCF figure for SpaceX must be derived (OCF − Capex), and the filing does not present or endorse this derivation.

3. **Segment Adjusted EBITDA ≠ Operating Cash Flow.** Amazon's ratio uses GAAP operating cash flow. SpaceX's Connectivity Segment Adjusted EBITDA is a non-GAAP measure that adds back depreciation & amortization, share-based compensation, restructuring, and impairments — none of which are cash-neutral. EBITDA systematically overstates cash generation relative to OCF, making the SpaceX ratio look less extreme than a true cash-based comparison. The consolidated OCF figures confirm this: Q1 2026 consolidated OCF was $1,047M vs. Connectivity Adjusted EBITDA of $2,087M (a ~50% gap), and FY2025 consolidated OCF was $6,785M vs. Connectivity Adjusted EBITDA of $7,168M.

4. **No segment-level operating cash flow disclosure.** The filing does not break out operating cash flow by segment. We cannot isolate the actual cash generated by Connectivity (as opposed to its EBITDA), nor can we determine how much of consolidated OCF is consumed by the loss-making Space and AI segments before any cash reaches the AI capex line.

5. **The question isolates AI capex, but SpaceX's total capex is far larger.** Using only AI capex ($30.9B annualized) understates total capex pressure. Total Q1 2026 capex annualized is $40.4B, which — measured against either Connectivity EBITDA or consolidated OCF — produces even more extreme ratios (484% and 965%, respectively).

6. **Annualization of a single quarter is an assumption the filing does not endorse.** Q1 2026 capex may not be representative of the full year. The filing notes SpaceX "may reduce future capital expenditures in this segment and reallocate those expenditures to other segments based on business priorities," implying the run-rate is discretionary, not committed.

7. **The consolidated financials are retrospectively recast** to include xAI (merged February 2, 2026) and X Holdings (merged into xAI March 28, 2025) for all historical periods. No period reflects standalone SpaceX operations. The AI segment's capex and losses reflect this recast entity, complicating any trend interpretation.

8. **No quantification of the internal-vs-external funding split for AI.** While the filing discloses aggregate debt and equity proceeds, it does not present a sources-and-uses table isolating how much AI capex was funded internally versus externally. This omission prevents a precise apples-to-apples comparison with Amazon's predominantly internally-funded capex.

**Bottom line:** On every reasonable construction, SpaceX's AI infrastructure funding pressure ratio (370%–965% depending on denominator) vastly exceeds Amazon's 94.5%, and SpaceX's position is materially more constrained and more dependent on external capital. However, the comparison's confidence is limited because (a) the S-1 does not designate Connectivity as the primary internal cash source, (b) it provides no free cash flow metric, (c) Segment Adjusted EBITDA is not a cash measure comparable to Amazon's OCF, and (d) no segment-level operating cash flow is disclosed.
